title: Puffy Eyes After Botox？ How to Diagnose & Treat Periorbital Edema.
filename: Puffy Eyes After Botox？ How to Diagnose & Treat Periorbital Edema..en.vtt
excerpt: A common problem all injectors will face at some point is perorbital edema after botuly toxin. It's essential that you know what to do, what to say to your patients, and how to handle this situation in the future.

segments.text: A common problem all injectors will face at some point is perorbital edema after botuly toxin. It's essential that you know what to do, what to say to your patients, and how to handle this situation in the future. If you'd like to learn how to handle perorbital edema, this video is especially for you. Actually, it was especially for Caroline who's one of our members. If you'd like to join my membership, see the description down below. We have a question from Caroline who treated her patients with moderate dose of botime toxin only using two full and two units in each injection site around the eyes but then her patients suffered from about a month worth of edema. So what is going on and what should we do in these circumstances? The first thing is to understand the time course of this. This seems to happen in quite a predictable way in my experience, which is the patient is typically okay and then they get swelling on day three to four that gets worse peaking at around 2 weeks before gradually getting better and usually fully resolving by about a month. I'm not sure exactly why it flows this way and whether it is always this way with patients, but I've had many of

these. This tends to be the path that it takes. There are two things I think that could be causing this. Edema is caused by a buildup of fluid. And the theory is that as the orvicular isoculi is relaxed, the contraction of that muscle is partly responsible for squeezing fluid out of the eye. So with a relaxed muscle, you get a buildup of fluid and that buildup of fluid peaks around 2 weeks before perhaps the lymphatics get a little bit broader and allow the fluid to flow back out again. This is all extremely theoretical. I don't think anyone actually knows. The other theory is that this is somewhat of a reaction to one of the components of the bottom toxin injections. Maybe it's the botchime toxin itself, but there's also lactulose and human albine included in the exipients and maybe some patients react to that. What's particularly confusing is that you may find that if you switch toxins and treat the same patient with a different toxin, they do not get the same result. So they often do not get edema with a different product. Or sometimes you can switch that same patient back, do exactly the same procedure and still not get perorbital edema. Or worse still, you

can treat them and it's even worse the second time. All of these situations have arisen in my clinic, but all of them cast a little bit of doubt on what is actually going on. It seems quite hard to figure out a clear pattern that will tell you for sure what's causing it and what you could do to treat it. However, there are some very safe things that I think you can do with your patients. The first thing is tell them about the time course. Although you have a little bit of swelling and it may be down to a little bit of inflammation from the procedure or a buildup of fluid due to decreased contractility of the muscle that it will probably pass on its own within a month. You can of course treat them with antihistamines to address the potential risk that it is partly inflammatory. And finally, you can also give them exercise to help them squeeze fluid out of their eyes. So 5 minutes each morning of a compress over the eyes just to squeeze the fluid will increase the rate at which fluid leaves the tissues and let them get on with a normal day sooner than if you did nothing at
